EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of heavy sleet and significant freezing rain fell over North Central and Central Oregon.  Sleet accumulations of 1-1.5 inches fell six miles west of Wamic, Kah-nee-ta, and seven miles east of Grass Valley.  Ice accumulations of .75 inches was observed near Redmond, with a quarter inch ice accumulation near Warm Springs and Alfalfa.

A mix of heavy sleet and significant freezing rain fell over North Central and Central Oregon.  Sleet accumulations of 1-1.5 inches fell six miles west of Wamic, Kah-nee-ta, and seven miles east of Grass Valley.  Ice accumulations of .75 inches was observed near Redmond, with a quarter inch ice accumulation near Warm Springs and Alfalfa.
A mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain fell in the Blue Mountain Foothills of Oregon.  Heppner received a quarter inch accumulation of ice, and the Pendleton Airport received 1.3 inches of snow.

Light freezing drizzle in the Blue Mountain Foothills of Oregon led to extremely icy roadways on interstate 84.  The icy roadways led to nine ice-related crashes.  One car spun out of control on an icy stretch, and struck an officer attending to a rollover accident, resulting in one direct injury.

A combination of snow and blowing snow closed interstate 84 in the Grande Ronde Valley late in the afternoon on the 7th through 1:30 AM on the 8th.  The La Grande AWOS reported a peak wind gust of 52 MPH at 9:15 PM PST.  A trained spotter in the town of Union measured four inches of snow.

Freezing drizzle fell in the Blue Mountain Foothills of Oregon, leading to very icy roadways.  Temperatures remained below freezing through the day on the 4th which resulted in icy roadways all day.  Schools in Pendleton and Milton-Freewater began classes two hours late.  Oregon State Police responded to 15 crashes on interstate 84 due to icy conditions on the 4th.
A strong wind gust at Eagles Hot Lake RV Resort SE of La Grande blew over a RV trailer, severly damaging the trailer.  The property damage from this event is estimated at $10,000.

Several crashes occurred on interstate 84 after freezing rain coated roadways with ice.  One accident four miles west of Pendleton on interstate 84 resulted in one indirect injury.  Oregon State Police also responded to several crashes near Meacham, but most were slide-off and non-injury crashes.

A mixture of snow and sleet fell in the Lower Columbia Basin in Oregon.  A weather spotter 12 miles south of Arlington reported a sleet accumulation of a half inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: See Idaho event this date.

A moisture-laden Pacific storm rode over cold air pouring out of the Columbia River Gorge, resulting in widespread moderate to heavy freezing rain in NW Oregon. Accumulations ranged from one quarter to one half inch, with heaviest amounts near the Columbia River Gorge. The brunt of the storm occurred January 15 into the early part of January 16, but the storm lasted two additional days in the Gorge. The Central and Southern Willamette Valley had the least amount of freezing rain, and it turned to liquid rain by the afternoon of January 15. The storm forced closure of bridges around the Portland metropolitan area and Interstate 5 was closed from Wilsonville to south of Salem for 3 hours on the 15th. The storm also resulted in cancellation of 225 flights from Portland International Airport, 5000 power outages, and over 200 motor vehicle accidents. Numerous trees were toppled by ice accumulation, mainly near the Gorge. A woman of unknown age was killed when she was hit by a car after jumping out of her automobile when it began to slide. Area hospitals reported 49 injuries due to people falling on ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the NW Oregon Coast Range, Northern Oregon Cascades, and Columbia River Gorge. In the Coast Range 8 inches fell in Buxton, 5 inches west of McMinnville, and 4 inches at Sunset Summit and Wilson River Summit. In the Cascades 10 to 12 inches fell on Mt Hood, and 9 inches at Santiam Junction. In the Gorge Cascade Locks reported 2 inches and Hood River had 1 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell in the NW Oregon Coast Range, with 8 inches in Buxton, 5 inches west of McMinnville, and 4 inches at Sunset Summit and Wilson River Summit.

A moisture-laden Pacific storm rode over cold air pouring out of the Columbia River Gorge, resulting in widespread moderate to heavy freezing rain in NW Oregon. A tree snapped at its base and fell onto several parked cars at Westside Elementary School in Madras.  The high winds also downed some trees onto road and utility lines in Camp Sherman.  The wind gusts downed some tree limbs and caused power outages near Warm Springs.  A metal storage shed was also blown over due to the winds.  A greenhouse was demolished by the winds.  A wind gust of 62 MPH was recorded near Opal Springs in Jefferson County at 12:45 PM PST.  The property damage from this event is estimated at $6,000.

High winds caused blowing dust which reduced visibilities to near zero on interstate 84 west of Pendleton.  Vehicles were reported pulling off the road.  In addition, four miles north of Heppner on State Route 207, blowing dust reduced visibilities to near zero.

High winds near The Dalles blew shingles off of a roof.  An ODOT sensor five miles east-northeast of Rufus recorded a wind gust of 64 MPH at 6:46 PM PST, followed by another strong gust of 58 MPH at 7:01 PM PST.

Strong winds caused blowing dust and reduced visibilities to near zero in spots on interstate 84 between Boardman and Echo.  Some vehicles were pulling off the road.  Traffic slowed to 25 MPH on the interstate due to the reduced visibility and tumbleweeds blowing across the road.  In addition, on highway 207 near Hermiston visibility was reduced to near zero due to blowing dust.  The extremely low visibility contributed to a non-injury collision near the Boardman Bombing Range.
Motorists on Highway 97 north of Madras reported visibilities down to near zero at times.

At Black Butte Ranch, high winds uprooted trees which fell onto roofs of a couple homes and a car at the Ranch.  High winds three miles west of Alfalfa contributed to a three foot tree splitting down the middle, with half of the tree landing on a porch.   In the city of Bend, a tree flattened three cars and a pickup in a lot on Emkay Street. The winds knocked down several trees and caused power outages near Sunriver.  Power outages were also reported in Sisters and at the Black Butte Ranch for as long as six hours.  Fall River Fish Hatchery Road was impassible due to a downed tree across the road.  In La Pine, a garage roof was blown off.  The property damage from this event is estimated at $18,000.

A frontal system moving onto the coast brought high winds to the area. Yaquina Bridge reported gusts to 68 mph, Sea Lion Caves gusts to 76 mph, Cannon Beach gusts to 70 mph, Newport Jetty gusts to 60 mph and Cape Foulweather gusts to 59 mph.  Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported at the Newport and Astoria airports as well as in Florence.  High winds caused one wide spread outage and numerous smaller outages in the city of Clatskanie.  All outages were the result of trees or branches falling over distribution lines.  Nearly 800 customers were without electricity for over an hour.

High wind gusts fanned embers which ignited a nearby old barn off U.S. Highway 26 about five miles north of Madras.  Thick smoke from the fire caused highway 26 to be closed with traffic being diverted to Boise Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche was triggered on Thayer Glacier on North Sister, or 14 miles southwest of the city of Sisters.  Two climbers were caught in the avalanche and were seriously injured.  Two blackhawk helicopters from the Oregon National Guard airlifted the injured climbers off the mountain.

The Juniper Canyon Fire was a lightning caused fire burning an estimated 5,000 acres.  The fire burned north of South Juniper Canyon Road about five miles north of Holdman.  The fire was a sleeper fire after lightning that occurred the previous day.  Winds carried the blaze which burned in an area of steep terrain.  A total of 60-80 firefighters and 30 fire units from the county were called out to the blaze.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near the Columbia River Gorge dumped heavy rain that caused mud slides on the Historic Columbia River Gorge Scenic Highway.  A weather spotter in Troutdale reported one inch of rain in twenty minutes, and half-an-inch was reported at the Troutdale airport in an hour.  Eight slides closed the Scenic Highway for twenty-four hours while 1,000 cubic yards of debris were removed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms spread through the Willamette Valley, generating strong wind gusts that resulted in reports of downed trees and power lines and local damage. A large section of metal roof was peeled back between Lebanon and Sweet Home.
Near West Linn, OR a lightning strike left up to 1800 residents without power for just under an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with a plugged culvert led to flooding on a bridge at Minam, with rocks piling up on the bridge as well.  A nearby weather station at Point Prominence recorded 1.23 inches of rain from 7 pm on the 5th to 11 am on the 6th.  Minam Lodge received about .7 inches of precipitation during this same period.

High winds with gusts to 70 MPH occurred at the US 97 Junction in Biggs, OR.  An ODOT sensor also recorded a wind gust of 62 MPH on the John Day River Bridge on Interstate 84 at MP 114, about five miles east of Rufus.

Very heavy rain from a thunderstorm led to flash flooding on some streets in Bend, with some debris floating down roadways.  One vehicle became stranded at a flooded underpass in Bend.  Rainfall amounts from the storm totaled up to 1.25 inches, with .5 inches falling in a 10 minute period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced frequent lightning in the Eugene and Springfield area early in the morning.  Lightning struck several power transformers in Eugene and Cottage Grove, resulting in  power outages to 300 Eugene residences and 50 in Cottage Grove.

A particularly strong thunderstorm formed near the Cascades in Lane county and moved north through eastern Lane, Marion, and Clackamas counties during the evening, producing strong winds and frequent lightning. In Gresham a home was reportedly struck by lightning; no one was injured but there was extensive damage to the house. Lightning hit several transformers, and trees and limbs felled by wind downed power lines, resulting in loss of power service to approximately 4,000 customers in Gresham, Troutdale, and Sandy for most of the evening.  Eight miles east of Springfield 70 mph winds blew down numerous trees. Over an inch of rain fell in 45 minutes in the Eugene/Springfield area, resulting in local street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rattlesnake Springs fire, centered nine miles ESE of Warm Springs, was a lightning sparked fire which burned 1,170 acres.  No structures were lost in the blaze.
The Shitike Creek Fire, centered two miles WNW of Warm Springs, burned 412 acres.  There were no structures lost in the blaze.
The Mule Peak Fire, centered 19 miles ESE of Union, was a lightning caused fire which burned 1400 acres.  The fire continued to burn into the month of August.

A lightning sparked fire followed by gusty winds near 40 MPH occurred about 15 miles southwest of Echo.  This fire burned 1,050 acres of wheat, which was equivalent to 36,750 bushels priced at an estimated $3.10 per bushel.
The Wolfe Point Fire, centered near Kah-nee-ta, was a human caused fire and burned 4,150 acres.  No structures were lost in the blaze.
The Schoolie Rim Fire, centered four miles WNW of Kah-nee-ta, burned 1,271 acres.  There were no structures lost in the blaze.

A particularly intense area of thunderstorms moved over northwest Oregon and southwest Washington producing localized heavy rain and minor flooding.  The Troutdale airport recorded one-and-one-half inches of rain in one hour, and the Vancouver airport recorded more than three-quarters of an inch of rain during the event.  Minor flooding occurred over multiple areas, mainly on small neighborhood streets.  Heavy rain over the Historic Columbia River Highway did cause a minor landslide and debris flow, but the road remained open as the debris was cleaned up.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 38-foot tuna charter boat capsized near the Umpqua River Jetty in Winchester Bay, off of Reedsport, Oregon.  The Coast Guard rescued the vessel captain and a passenger.  Two others were found dead in the water, and a fifth person remains missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chicken House Fire, centered nine miles SSE of Dayville, was a lightning caused fire which burned 618 acres.  The fire started on July 31st.
The Mule Peak Fire, centered 19 miles ESE of Union, was a lightning caused fire which burned 1400 acres.  The fire started on July 28th.
The Dry Cabin Fire, centered 10 miles southeast of Dayville, was a lightning caused fire which burned 270 acres.  The fire started on July 28th.

The Troutdale airport recorded one-and-one-half inches of rain in one hour, and the Vancouver airport recorded more than three-quarters of an inch of rain during the event.  Minor flooding occurred over multiple areas, mainly on small neighborhood streets.  Heavy rain over the Historic Columbia River Highway did cause a minor landslide and debris flow, but the road remained open as the debris was cleaned up.
The Burnt Cabin Fire, centered five miles north of Tollgate, burned 1,977 acres.  The fire originally started on August 14th.
The Awbrey Saddle Fire, centered six miles NNE of Grizzly, burned 128 acres.  The fire originally started on August 30th.
The Pine Stub Fire, centered six miles east of Post, was a human caused fire which burned 597 acres.

The Blalock Fire, centered 10 miles southeast of Milton-Freewater, burned 125 acres.  Two engines, two hotshot crews, one tender, and one dozer assisted in putting out the blaze.
The McKinley Fire, centered 12 miles SSW of Fossil, burned 3,811 acres.
The Spring Creek Fire, centered 13 miles southeast of Meacham, burned 1,165 acres.
The Burnt Cabin Fire, centered five miles north of Tollgate, burned 1,977 acres.  This fire burned into the month of September.
The Cave Fire, centered 12 miles southeast of Bend, burned 852 acres.
The Wills Canyon Fire, centered two miles ESE of Service Creek, was a lightning caused fire that burned 895 acres.
The Fly Fire, centered seven miles southeast of Lehman Hot Springs, was a lightning caused fire that burned 838 acres.
The Awbrey Saddle Fire, centered six miles NNE of Grizzly, burned 128 acres.  This fire burned into the month of September.
The Rinehart Road Fire, centered 15 miles northeast of La Grande, burned 670 acres.
The Tryon Complex Fire, centered 15 miles northeast of Imnaha, was a lightning caused fire that burned about 42,736 acres.
Based on the above report, a Tornado Warning was issued for the Christmas Valley area of Lake County at 21/1609 PDT, expiring at 21/1645 PDT.  No reports of damage, injuries, or deaths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake County 911 called and relayed a report from a woman in Christmas Valley of several funnel clouds, one of which was touching the ground.  The 911 dispatcher passed along her phone number.  The woman was called and a tornado was verified.  She stated that there were several funnel clouds extending down from a cloud, and one of the funnel clouds was touching the ground with a dust cloud at the point of contact.  She estimated the tornado was 3 miles west of Christmas Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Based on the above spotter report, a Severe Thunderstorm Warning was issued for extreme north central Klamath County at 21/1621 PDT and expired at 21/1645 PDT. A wildfire near Helix bruned 150 acres of wheat and stubble, with about 75 acres each.  A harvest truck was also burned in the blaze which was holding a full load of 8 to 10 tons of wheat.  The property damage from this event is estimated at $5,000, and the crop damage from this event is estimated at $23,000.
A wildfire burned 300 to 400 acres of wheat on the North Fork of the Walla Walla River and Power Line Road five miles east of Milton-Freewater.  Estimated crop damage from this event is $83,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Applegate River at Wilderville exceeded flood stage (13.0 feet) at 30/1630 PST, crested at 13.76 feet at 30/1900 PST, and fell below flood stage at 31/0030 PST.  A Flood Warning was issued for the area at 30/1817 PST, and cancelled at 31/0440 PST.

The Umpqua River at Elkton exceeded flood stage (33.0 feet) at 30/2200 PST, crested at 39.54 feet at 31/0600 PST, and fell below flood stage at 01/0430 PST in January 2006.  A Flood Warning was issued for the area at 30/1215 PST, and cancelled at 01/1103 in January 2006.

High winds blew down approximately eight trees and a stop sign in the Joseph area.  Several power poles were snapped under the force of the wind two miles east of Joseph on the Imnaha highway.  Power outages were also reported due to the wind, including Joseph High School.  In Enterprise, two inch diameter tree limbs were snapped due to high winds.  An automated weather station in Joseph recorded a peak wind of 58 MPH.
A strong wind gust blew over a Ponderosa Pine Tree which fell on two mobile homes causing extensive damage at Sisters Mobile Home Park.  The property damage from this event is estimated at $40,000.
Heavy snow fell in La Pine where 4.5 inches of snow had accumlated at 1005 PM.  The snow began falling during the evening hours around 7 pm.
A wind gust of 60 MPH occurred in Huston Canyon, 16 miles west-southwest of Heppner.
Sustained wind speeds of 39 MPH with gusts to 46 MPH blew a few shingles off a barn.
Two windows were broken due to strong wind gusts in Pilot Rock.
Ice accumulations of a quarter inch were observed in Condon and 10 miles northwest of Hardman.  The weight of the ice knocked down several small tree branches in Condon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from afternoon thunderstorms on the White River Glacier on Mt. Hood, aided by what appeared to be a glacial outflow, resulted in heavy runoff on the White River near Oregon Highway 35 between Parkdale and the White River Snow Park. The river came out of its channel, created a new channel, and flowed across the highway. Oregon Department of Transportation crews closed the highway for several days in order to clean debris from the roadway. Heavy rain from thunderstorms also resulted in record daily rainfall amounts at a number of locations in the Portland metropolitan area. Amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches, with Troutdale airport reporting 2.09 inches.

A very wet series of Pacific systems moved through northwest Oregon and southwest Washington dropping copious amounts of rainfall over the area.  During the period of time, between 2-3 inches of precipitation fell over the Willamette Valley, and between 4-5 inches fell over parts of the Coast and Cascade Ranges.  This period of heavy rainfall pushed many rivers above bankfull, and most of those above flood stage.  Many roads around the area were reported closed due to high water, and road workers were busy battling several landslides.  Here is a list of rivers in Oregon which flooded during the period: Pudding River at Aurora: flood stage: 22.0 feet, crest: 25.9 feet on 01/01/06; Clackamas River nr Estacada: flood stage 20.0 feet, crest: 21.9 feet on 12/31/05; Tualatin River nr Dilley: flood stage: 17.5 feet, crest: 17.6 feet on 12/29/05, crest: 17.7 feet on 12/30/05; Tualatin River at Farmington: flood stage: 32.0 feet, crest: 32.9 on 12/31/05; Willamette River at Harrisburg: flood stage: 14.0 feet, crest: 14.2 feet on 12/31/05; Willamette River blw Falls at Oregon City: flood stage: 27.0 feet, crest: 27.5 feet on 01/02/06; S Santiam River nr Jefferson: flood stage: 15.0 feet, crest: 15.9 feet on 12/31/05; Siuslaw River at Mapleton: flood stage: 18.0 feet, crest: 19.7 on 12/28/05, crest 24.0 feet on 12/30/05; Marys River nr Philomath: flood stage: 20.0 feet, crest: 20.7 feet on 12/28/05, crest: 21.2 feet on 12/30/05; Mohawk River nr Springfield: flood stage: 15.0 feet, crest: 18.0 feet on 12/31/05; Luckiamute River nr Suver: flood stage: 27.0 feet, crest: 28.0 feet on 12/29/05, crest: 29.9 feet on 12/31/05; Alsea River nr Tidewater: flood stage: 18.0 feet, crest: 21.8 feet on 12/30/05.

A strong winter storm brought a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to lower elevations of northwest Oregon.  Several reports were received of sleet and heavy freezing rain blanketing the area.  1-3" of snow fell in Portland, 4" in Sandy, 3-6" in Cascade Locks, and 6" near Hood River.  Multiple damage reports were received due to ice accumulating on tree branches and power lines, especially in areas near the Columbia River Gorge.  Ice delayed crews from restoring power to nearly 9,000 east Multnomah county residents.  Ice on the tracks of the local light rail system also prevented trains from reaching east Multnomah county.

A very wet series of Pacific systems moved through northwest Oregon and southwest Washington dropping copious amounts of rainfall over the area.  During the period of time, between 2-3 inches of precipitation fell over the Willamette Valley, and between 4-5 inches fell over parts of the Coast and Cascade Ranges.  This period of heavy rainfall pushed many rivers above bankfull, and most of those above flood stage.  Many roads around the area were reported closed due to high water, and road workers were busy battling several landslides.  Here is a list of rivers in Oregon which flooded during the period: Pudding River at Aurora: flood stage: 22.0 feet, crest: 25.9 feet on 01/01/06; Clackamas River nr Estacada: flood stage 20.0 feet, crest: 21.9 feet on 12/31/05; Tualatin River nr Dilley: flood stage: 17.5 feet, crest: 17.6 feet on 12/29/05, crest: 17.7 feet on 12/30/05; Tualatin River at Farmington: flood stage: 32.0 feet, crest: 32.9 on 12/31/05; Willamette River at Harrisburg: flood stage: 14.0 feet, crest: 14.2 feet on 12/31/05; Willamette River blw Falls at Oregon City: flood stage: 27.0 feet, crest: 27.5 feet on 01/02/06; S Santiam River nr Jefferson: flood stage: 15.0 feet, crest: 15.9 feet on 12/31/05; Siuslaw River at Mapleton: flood stage: 18.0 feet, crest: 19.7 on 12/28/05, crest 24.0 feet on 12/30/05; Marys River nr Philomath: flood stage: 20.0 feet, crest: 20.7 feet on 12/28/05, crest: 21.2 feet on 12/30/05; Mohawk River nr Springfield: flood stage: 15.0 feet, crest: 18.0 feet on 12/31/05; Luckiamute River nr Suver: flood stage: 27.0 feet, crest: 28.0 feet on 12/29/05, crest: 29.9 feet on 12/31/05; Alsea River nr Tidewater: flood stage: 18.0 feet, crest: 21.8 feet on 12/30/05.

A very wet series of Pacific systems moved through northwest Oregon and southwest Washington dropping copious amounts of rainfall over the area.
